books.title: The Old Man and the Sea
books.author: Ernest Hemingway
books.context: I came to this after abandoning Henry James at chapter 15. The Portrait of a Lady left me cold ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” characters hooked to their ideas, nobody real, nobody moving. I needed a calibration reset. A man alone with his craft. No noise. This is what I found.

themes.title: Age as clarification
themes.body: I keep returning to what gets stripped away with age and what stays. Pride goes first ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” it becomes a luxury I can no longer afford. Humility is not weakness; it is the actual legs I walk on now. When I read about the old man's eyes ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” unchanged, cheerful, undefeated ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” I felt seen. The soul holds its life better than the flesh does.

themes.title: Memory and distillation
themes.body: I question my own memories the older I get. Did I remember that, or did someone tell me? But I have come to think accuracy is not really the point. What matters is what gets distilled ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” what remains after the rest falls away. That is what memoir is, for me. Not the record. The residue.

themes.title: Dreams and ownership
themes.body: The old man no longer dreams of storms or women or great fish. He dreams of places. I read that and thought: this is what maturity looks like in the interior life. No more wanting things that can be owned. The bigger themes ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” those can never be owned, only shared.

themes.title: The Swiss knife of age
themes.body: I made a list of what the old man actually has: luck, humility, experience, trickery, mysticism, resolution, empathy, sympathy, and lions in dreams. That is not a catalogue of losses. That is a toolkit. I recognise most of those things in myself now, and I did not have them when I was young.

themes.title: The big fish
themes.body: Would he go out at all if there were no fish to hope for? I did not answer that when I read it. I still have not answered it. It is the question underneath everything ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” about what keeps a person moving, what faith actually is when you strip the word down.

entries.quote: Everything about him was old except his eyes and they were the same color as the sea and were cheerful and undefeated.
entries.response: And the eyes are the doors to the soul, it changes and matures but it holds its life better than the flesh..

entries.quote: Can you really remember that or did I just tell it to you? / I remember everything from when we first went together.
entries.response: The younger you are, the more sure about you memories. The older I become I question the memory from the memoirs.. but does it matter?! Not really, it's what is distilled that matters..

entries.quote: Thank you, the old man said. He was too simple to wonder when he had attained humility. But he knew he had attained it and he knew it was not disgraceful...
entries.response: Wow.. how true.. Pride is a luxury for the young, humility the legs of aging.. life do nibble on pride, a survival mechanism is it?!

entries.quote: He no longer dreamed of storms, nor of women, nor of great occurrences, nor of great fish, nor fights, nor contests of strength, nor of his wife. He only dreamed of places...
entries.response: Of man and his dreams.. no more for wants and ownerships.. it's dreams of the bigger themes that can never be owned, but shared.

entries.quote: My big fish must be somewhere.
entries.response: The hope of a big fish, would he go out if it's none..

entries.quote: I am a strange old man. / But are you strong enough now for a truly big fish? / I think so. And there are many tricks.
entries.response: Let's count the Swiss knife tools of the aged as Hemingway thinks; luck, humility, experience, trickery, mysticism, resolution, empathy, sympathy and lions in dreams.

books.title: The Heart's Invisible Furies
books.author: John Boyne
books.context: Recommended to me as East of Eden crossed with Covenant of Water ГѓВѓГ‚ВўГѓВ‚Г‚ВЂГѓВ‚Г‚В” modern, sharper, faster. Life across decades. Identity, family, exile, belonging. I started it after Hemingway.

themes.title: Religious Hypocrisy and Moral Contradictions
themes.body: I'm struck by how Boyne immediately exposes the gap between religious authority and moral behavior. The priest who should embody virtue is the one committing the sins, while the community that claims goodness shows none of it. This irony sets up what I expect will be a sustained critique of institutional morality throughout the novel.

themes.title: The Power of Sardonic Storytelling
themes.body: I love how Boyne uses sarcasm as a narrative weapon right from the start. That capitalized 'Good People of Goleen' feels like a bitter joke that the narrator is sharing with me. It's a storytelling technique that makes me complicit in the criticismГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”I'm in on the joke about these supposedly good people who are anything but.

themes.title: Social Judgment and Shame as Weapons
themes.body: I can already see how shame and social condemnation will be major forces in this story. The way the community turns on the mother while protecting or ignoring the priest's behavior tells me everything about how power and gender dynamics work in this setting. I suspect this opening scene of public shaming will echo throughout the entire narrative.

themes.title: The Corruption of Innocence
themes.body: What strikes me about this opening is how it immediately corrupts any notion of innocence or goodness. Even the chapter title becomes tainted with irony, and I'm left questioning whether anyone in this story will truly embody the virtue they claim to represent. It's a brilliant way to prepare me for a world where moral certainties don't exist.

themes.title: The Circular Nature of Time and Return
themes.body: I'm fascinated by how Boyne uses time as both a linear progression and a circle that eventually closes. The image of someone returning after sixty years to find only graveyards where judgment once lived speaks to me about the futility of exile and the way time becomes the ultimate equalizer. I see this as both melancholic and satisfyingГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”the idea that we outlast our persecutors simply by surviving.

themes.title: Humor as Survival Mechanism
themes.body: I find myself drawn to how Boyne uses comedy to make tragedy bearable, much like McCourt did. The way he can make me laugh at situations that should devastate me shows me how humor becomes a form of resistance against cruelty. I think this sardonic tone isn't just stylisticГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”it's how people actually cope with impossible circumstances, turning pain into something they can control through laughter.

themes.title: The Irony of Titles and Labels
themes.body: I'm struck by how Boyne plays with the gap between what people call themselves and what they actually are. 'The Good People of Goleen' becomes immediately ironic when none of them demonstrate goodness, and I see this pattern throughout the book. I think he's showing me how society uses these labels as masks, and how the most dangerous people are often those who wear the most virtuous titles.

themes.title: Geography as Character and Memory
themes.body: I notice how places in this novel aren't just settingsГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”they're repositories of memory and judgment that persist across decades. Goleen becomes a character itself, one that remembers shame and exile long after the people who created them are gone. I see the village as representing all the small, suffocating places we flee from, only to realize they've shaped us more than we ever wanted to admit.

entries.quote: The Good People of Goleen Long before we discovered that he had fathered two children by two different women, one in Drimoleague and one in Cl...
entries.response:
I think this has to count as one of the most memorable first paragraphs of a novel .. it starts with a sarcastic title of a chapter, followed by the act of a sinful priest, and ends with a mother called a whore!.
The Good People of GoleenГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”capitalizedГѓВўГ‚ВЂГ‚В”but no one seems to be Good.

entries.quote: Good luck to you now. And with that she took her leave of Goleen, the place of her birth, which she would not see again for more than sixty years, whe...
entries.response: The ending paragraph , tells us with what to come .. the cast away will return and probably nothing would have changed in the village but for the occupied graveyard housing the people who shunned her 60 years earlier. The whole chapter feels more comic than tragic , the humour echoes the style of Frank McCourt in Angela's ashes.

reflection.text:
I'm barely past the opening, but already I can feel Boyne pulling me into something that's both sharper and more sprawling than I expected. That first chapter hit me like a slapГўВЂВ”the way he opens with "The Good People of Goleen" in such formal capitals, only to immediately demolish any notion of goodness. It's almost theatrical, this contrast between the chapter's dignified title and the mess of human failing that spills out underneath. A priest with illegitimate children, a young woman cast out and called a whoreГўВЂВ”and yet somehow I found myself laughing more than wincing.

What's catching me off guard is how Boyne manages to make this opening feel both devastating and oddly buoyant. When I noted the similarity to Frank McCourt's style, I think I was grasping at something about the toneГўВЂВ”this way of treating terrible circumstances with a kind of knowing humor that doesn't diminish their weight but somehow makes them bearable. The girl being banished from Goleen should be purely tragic, but there's something in the storytelling that suggests resilience, even triumph.

That ending paragraph is haunting me now. The promise that she won't return for sixty years, and when she does, presumably everyone who shunned her will be safely dead and buriedГўВЂВ”there's something almost satisfying about that revenge of time. It makes me think this whole sweeping story is going to be about outlasting judgment, about the long arc of a life that extends beyond the small cruelties of small people.

I keep coming back to that phrase "The Good People of Goleen" because it feels like it's setting up the entire book's relationship with morality and social respectability. Already I sense that Boyne is going to spend the next several hundred pages showing me who the truly good people are, and I suspect they won't be the ones who think of themselves that way. There's something brewing here about the difference between appearing good and being good, and I have the feeling this exile from Goleen is just the beginning of a much larger reckoning with what we owe each other as human beings.

snapshots.text:
I'm barely past the opening, but already I can feel Boyne pulling me into something that's both sharper and more sprawling than I expected. That first chapter hit me like a slapГўВЂВ”the way he opens with "The Good People of Goleen" in such formal capitals, only to immediately demolish any notion of goodness. It's almost theatrical, this contrast between the chapter's dignified title and the mess of human failing that spills out underneath. A priest with illegitimate children, a young woman cast out and called a whoreГўВЂВ”and yet somehow I found myself laughing more than wincing.

What's catching me off guard is how Boyne manages to make this opening feel both devastating and oddly buoyant. When I noted the similarity to Frank McCourt's style, I think I was grasping at something about the toneГўВЂВ”this way of treating terrible circumstances with a kind of knowing humor that doesn't diminish their weight but somehow makes them bearable. The girl being banished from Goleen should be purely tragic, but there's something in the storytelling that suggests resilience, even triumph.

That ending paragraph is haunting me now. The promise that she won't return for sixty years, and when she does, presumably everyone who shunned her will be safely dead and buriedГўВЂВ”there's something almost satisfying about that revenge of time. It makes me think this whole sweeping story is going to be about outlasting judgment, about the long arc of a life that extends beyond the small cruelties of small people.

I keep coming back to that phrase "The Good People of Goleen" because it feels like it's setting up the entire book's relationship with morality and social respectability. Already I sense that Boyne is going to spend the next several hundred pages showing me who the truly good people are, and I suspect they won't be the ones who think of themselves that way. There's something brewing here about the difference between appearing good and being good, and I have the feeling this exile from Goleen is just the beginning of a much larger reckoning with what we owe each other as human beings.
